body.notes: Participation in IETF meetings constitutes agreement to the Note Well, which permits recording, transcription, and public archival of proceedings. See also BCP 78 (RFC 5378) and BCP 79 (RFC 8179) for IPR policies.

longer than we have to oh hi everyone welcome to the oblivious HTTP application intermediation working group session at ietf 116. we are your chairs Siobhan and Richard uh this is yeah this is the IIT of node well please do note it well um by participating in the ITF you are agreeing to follow ITF processes and policies and code of conduct so please do read that at some point um some general notes has been submitted for publication it is in the RFC editor queue and there was a few discusses that the authors worked through and thanks for them for that and yeah I think congrats congrats to the working group for for doing this yeah um big thanks to the authors for working through some complicated yep oh thanks Richard and yeah I think we can begin um Tommy did you want to present yes um [Music] it says you have control yeah try again all right good afternoon everyone so as we know our core Ojai documents are ships so uh and we'll be talking about our other adopted document which is about uh one of the discovery mechanisms that we can use for Ojai of specifically how we can discover the gateways and I'll Jump Right In great so as a recap to what this is doing um this is for a model where we have some Target service that we're trying to access as an oblivious Target resource that has an Associated oblivious Gateway and a client would like to discover that this target has an Associated Gateway the client is assumed to already have or already know about an oblivious relay and one of the constraints of this model that came out through the discussion and the analysis was that the Gateway in the target for this type of Discovery essentially have to be co-located or at least have their resources on the same origin so recently we did make a round of updates for this um right before the draft deadline for ITF it was based mainly on the things that we discussed last time so these things should not be hugely new one was a lovely bike shed we renamed the svcb parameter and all of the other strings to ohgp they used to be oblivious but ohtp works better and is also more consistent with the core dock um so yes this influenced the well-known URI and then the other major thing is that we clarified the behavior for uh using oblivious HTTP for DNS so I'll recap I'll go into a little bit more detail of this so here are the examples we had shared these before but now they're updated so they have in green these strings that are ohttp instead of oblivious so again a lovely bike shed but just so we walk through what we actually are doing here we have a DNS record that we get back when we're looking up a particular service and along with other things like the alpn it can have a parameter that says I support ohdp which means that there is a gateway associated with this and so this can happen for an https record it can happen for an svcb record such as an svcb record that is describing a DNS resolver so once you have that information the client could look up the well they can derive the location of the oblivious Gateway and they can also look up the key configuration so the oblivious Gateway is defined as a well-known URI off of the same origin as of the same host as the target service that you're trying to access and this Gateway it could you know redirect you or something but it has to be owned by that same origin so we can be sure that it actually is a safe thing to use and then to get the key configuration we have a way to uh also go find a well-known location to look up the keys so then the other major clarification was around the DNS Behavior so this is the model where you have oh HTTP um being used to transport dough and so this is do um which I yeah or do Duo I think we actually should pronounce it Duo um is the the new mechanism here similar to Odo but it's a different spelling so it's Duo so essentially this is just a case where you have standard binary HTTP oblivious HTTP messages to the Gateway the content of those binary HTTP messages are do messages and so it's quite straightforward to just compose those two protocols and you can discover this using the dns.resolver.arpa which we've defined from the add working group so that's a great way to learn that the local network you're on and what whatever IP address you had configured for your DNS resolver also supports connecting over Duo and we did note that when you're doing this we still need to have all of the server a certificate validation that is required when we're discovering a DNS resolver in this way um and this I I believe it's actually a pretty straightforward thing to do since we have to fetch the key configuration anyway for the oblivious uh HTTP DNS server at the same time we can validate that it has the certificate that matches the requirements of a discovered DNS server so that's the changes that went into this most recent version you can read the diff yourself as well um so I guess you know as as a preview my next slide will be I think the main issue we have left is consistency I would love to hear if we have other issues beyond that but I think the major gap which we talk about a bit in the document but I think needs to be fleshed out further is how we have good models for consistency and there are two main areas of consistency that I think are relevant here first we have the key consistency so we do have the key for the oblivious Gateway and that we look up with the well-known URI um there are different approaches you could do here so we could have our double check type of approach where we um do like a get Style Reverse proxy potentially using the Bolivia's relay to find this and then we could also do like a forward proxy uh style and check that the key config we get in both manners is the same you could also just have a model where you check once directly with through like a connect proxy and then you also ask your relay hey what do you think the key config for this uh Target or for the Gateway is and see if it's the same um this I think all needs to be written out in more detail I would love to hear people's input on what we should do here Chris do you want to comment on this one yeah um uh in the Privacy pass meeting on Friday there's going to be a section dedicated to key consistency for that particular protocol I imagine whatever comes of that particular work would equally apply here so I don't know if you're looking for a specific like standardized mechanism or more like considerations for consistency I think that is a question of for mechanisms like this in general has been met but like that doesn't mean like that won't change either below the bar or the bar will be met eventually um or or might not but let's just maybe wait a bit and see how things go sure um uh hi Alex Strahovski I wanted to ask if there is a assumption in your draft about the size of the deployments because it listen to the issues that were just brought up it sort of sounds to me like there's an implicit or possibly explicit assumption that the uh oblivious Gateway is deployed at larger scale than the oblivious Target and it's not obvious to me that that's actually going to be true in general because if you go and look at regular HTTP resources today without looking at any of the oblivious stuff it's usually the responsibility of every Target to somehow defend itself against an Outlook service attacks so whether that means using like a first party CDN or a third-party CDN or something else it still seems to me that you would need to have the capability to defend yourself directly from attacks even if they were going through one of these gateways so my question is does that change here it still seems to me that like the target would need to have especially if it has also non-oblivia's resources quite substantial serving power so like does this draft actually in its current form do anything other than provide like an advisory signal to the gateways to if they're polite that they should do this because otherwise I don't even understand how in the trust relationships that you were describing earlier how if it was done like as a ISP operator is that anyone would do anything other than best effort follow this it doesn't seem like there's any way to enforce it without having the target be able to sink any attack and at that point the target's syncing the attack so I'm I'm struggling a little bit to understand how the ecosystem concerns would actually turn into an effective deployment of this right now yeah in typical Target deployments like you say right targets do have uh firewalls to defend them from various types of attacks right with cdns absorbing details attacks at very large scale right that's a default deployment right one of the default policies to basically identify the attacker and then basically create apples to basically block that Target and all of that techniques but with with the anonymization that's happening it's some of those mitigations are no longer possible so this is acting as a way to see if this feedback would be taken and the policy could be enforcement but if if the relay is really is Loosely coupled it may not even enforce these policies and and see we could imagine an impact on the service that it is providing to the clients if the policy is not enforce it's not it's it's not a strict policy right I mean there's no feedback back from the relay back to the Target whether the rate limit is applied or not so um David proxy and relay Enthusiast um I I'm struggling a little bit with this draft because I find that on one hand it is kind of too vague and it's not clear like exactly when this is needed and like you know what assumptions it's having about the size of the deployment the types of deployment and on the other hand it's also a bit too specific to http and too specific to rate limiting like there are many other aspects that like if you're running a mask proxy or regular connect proxy you can have similar problems where there is abuse or dos or anything going through them and having maybe a way to tell them that there's a problem might be useful but to kind of go back to Chris Wood's Point um we haven't so like our on my Google we have a partnership with fast leader user HTTP for things like safe browsing and we haven't seen the need for this yet and so my gut feeling would be to say like not throw this out definitely not I I can see this being useful in the future but not yet and once we get there we might learn that maybe it's not right limiting that we wanted something else and other things so that would be my my personal opinion would be to not adopt at this time but not in a way to tell you that we're never going to but it feels a bit too early right now well thank you David for the excellent segue to the uh wrap-up I was going to try and do here um so Sean and I have been chatting in the background um it's kind of sounding to us like the authors here
